title: Easter biscuits
subtitle: Currant-and-spice shortcrust biscuits from the West Country
excerpt: Traditional British Easter biscuits from Somerset and the West Country: a spiced shortcrust enriched with egg and currants, rolled thick, cut with a round fluted cutter, and brushed with egg white then dusted with caster sugar after baking.
sourceNotes: Easter biscuits are a traditional West Country bake, particularly associated with Somerset and Gloucestershire. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records the currant-and-spice version and the tradition of making them at Easter. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, documents similar spiced currant biscuits. The cassia bark or mixed spice flavouring and the egg-white glaze with sugar are characteristic of the regional form.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough keeps in the refrigerator for 2 days. Baked biscuits keep in a tin for 5 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: A round biscuit cutter with a crimped, wavy edge rather than a plain straight one. Used for shortbread, custard creams, and traditional British biscuits such as Easter biscuits. A 7 cm fluted cutter is the standard size for Easter biscuits.

content.text: Easter biscuits are thicker and shorter than a shortbread finger, enriched with egg yolk rather than the classic all-butter method. The defining finish is an egg-white glaze brushed on just before the biscuits come out of the oven, then a heavy dusting of caster sugar that sets to a thin, sweet crust. The

Cream the butter and sugar until pale. Beat in the egg yolk. Add the mixed spice and lemon zest. Sift over the flour and mix to a dough, adding the currants and enough milk to bring everything together. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 20 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to 7–8 mm thick. Cut with a 7 cm fluted round cutter, re-rolling the scraps. Place on the trays. Bake for 12 minutes, then brush quickly with beaten egg white and dust generously with caster sugar. Return to the oven for 3 more minutes until pale gold and the sugar glaze is just set. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before moving to a rack.
